props.caption: Open weights spread as the strongest US models stay gated

items.note: The US-Iran stand-down held a second full day with no new confirmed strikes or tanker casualties. Technical talks on the mid-June memorandum convened in Doha through Qatari and Pakistani mediators; Washington and Tehran described the contact differently, and IAEA inspectors had not yet re-entered the struck sites.

Pakistani ground and air strikes hit eastern Afghan border districts overnight on 28–29 June. Islamabad said it killed fighters; the Taliban and UN monitors counted civilian dead. Kabul summoned Pakistan's envoy as the cross-border cycle deepened.

A week after the 24 June twin quakes, the confirmed toll passed 1,700 and kept rising, a fresh aftershock struck near La Guaira, and rescue crews worked against hollowed institutions and strained hospitals.

The Rapid Support Forces kept massing around El Obeid in North Kordofan amid warnings of mass-atrocity risk, with a UN urgent debate flagged for early July. The prevention window stayed open only because the city had not fallen.

items.what: A Bundibugyo-strain Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo could cost Africa up to $3.6 billion, the UN warned, as case and death counts climbed and response teams deployed.

South Korea's president unveiled a sweeping AI-chip investment drive backed by Samsung and SK Hynix, days after a ruling-party local-election sweep, deepening the state's bet on the semiconductor race.

The yen slid to a 40-year low against the dollar as intervention talk grew, while China's factory activity returned to expansion at 50.3, a rare fresh stabilization signal from the world's second-largest economy.

Europe's record heatwave drove French excess deaths past 1,000 for the week and strained power grids and transport, with attribution scientists calling the heat near-impossible without human-caused warming.
Serbia's president signaled he would step down and move toward early elections, an under-covered political turn in the Balkans after months of protest pressure.
An Indonesian court sentenced Gojek co-founder and former education minister Nadiem Makarim to 10 years in a graft case, a pointed test of elite accountability and the investment climate.
